questions.prompt: Match the clause: "If I had a car, ___"

options.text: I will drive to work.
I drive to work.
I would drive to work.
I would to drive to work.

questions.explanation: The if-clause "If I had…" is past simple, which signals the second conditional, so the result must be would + base verb: I would drive to work. "will drive" mixes in the first conditional, "I drive" drops the modal, and "would to drive" wrongly adds to after would.

options.text: If it will snow, we will stay home.
If you press this button, the machine stops.
If I had time, I would help you.
If she calls, I will answer.

questions.explanation: The error is (a): you must never put will in the if-clause — it should be If it snows, we will stay home. (b) is a correct zero conditional, (c) a correct second conditional, and (d) a correct first conditional.

questions.prompt: Complete: "If you ___ harder, you would get better marks."

options.text: will work
work
would work
worked

questions.explanation: The result "would get" tells us this is the second conditional, so the if-clause needs the past simple: If you worked harder… The modal goes in the result only, so "will work" and "would work" are wrong, and "work" (present) would not match "would get".

options.text: If the weather is nice, we would go hiking.
If the weather is nice, we will go hiking.
If the weather will be nice, we go hiking.
If the weather were nice, we will go hiking.

questions.explanation: A real future plan uses the first conditional: if + present simple, then will + base — If the weather is nice, we will go hiking. (a) wrongly uses would, (c) puts will in the if-clause, and (d) mixes a past if-clause with will.

questions.prompt: Complete the zero conditional: "If you ___ plants regularly, they grow well."

options.text: will water
would water
water
watered

questions.explanation: This is a general truth (zero conditional), so both clauses use the present simple: If you water plants regularly, they grow well. "will water" and "would water" wrongly add a modal, and "watered" is the past simple, not used for a permanent fact.

options.text: If I were you, I would see a doctor.
If you heat metal, it expands.
If we leave now, we will be on time.
If I won the prize, I will buy a house.

questions.explanation: The error is (d): it mixes conditionals — a past if-clause (won) needs would, not will, so it should be If I won the prize, I would buy a house. (a) is a correct second conditional, (b) a correct zero conditional, and (c) a correct first conditional.
